params.text:
When and why plans are reviewed

A care plan is a living document, so it must be reviewed to stay useful. A review is a planned check that the support still meets the person's needs and still helps them reach their outcomes. The Care Act 2014 expects plans to be kept under review.

  • Planned reviews: a regular check, often at least once a year, that the plan is still right.
  • Triggered reviews: a review brought forward because something has changed.
  • The person involved: the individual takes part in reviewing their own plan.

A review can confirm the plan is working, adjust it, or set new outcomes as the person's life changes.

Your part in reviews

Reviews are usually led by a senior worker or assessor, but they depend on information from the people who deliver the care. The observations you record between reviews — changes you noticed, outcomes reached, support that no longer fits — are exactly what a review draws on. Without them, a review has little to go on.

A review may be needed sooner than planned when a person's needs change noticeably: after a spell in hospital, a fall, a bereavement, or a steady decline or improvement. You may be asked to share what you have seen, and you should do so honestly and factually. Reviewing keeps a plan accurate and personalised over time, and your steady, everyday recording is what makes an honest review possible.

params.question: A care plan should be reviewed only once, when it is first written.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. A plan is a living document; it is reviewed regularly and again whenever the person's needs change.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. A care plan is a living document that must be reviewed regularly and whenever needs change.
